templates.name: Ultimate PRD
templates.description: A clean and structured Product Requirements Document (PRD) template for Notion. Includes sections for problem, vision, user cases, solution, requirements, metrics, and timeline. Perfect for aligning teams and documenting product development from idea to launch.
templates.body_text:
📝 Product Requirements Document (PRD) Template for Notion
This Notion template provides a complete, structured framework for creating and maintaining a Product Requirements Document (PRD). It’s designed to help product teams, founders, and stakeholders clearly define and align on what is being built, why, and how.
📦 What’s Inside
The template includes the following key sections:
🧩 Problem Statement
Identify the core user problems and unmet needs the product aims to solve. Helps clarify why this project matters and aligns everyone around a shared understanding of the pain points.
🎯 Vision & Goals
Define the product vision, long-term mission, and short-term deliverables. Includes a goal checklist to keep progress measurable and actionable.
👥 Target Users & Use Cases
Database-style table for documenting user personas and the jobs they want to accomplish. Captures user stories and prioritizes them by importance (P0–P2).
💡 Proposed Solution
Describe the proposed product or feature set. Outline the MVP scope and unique value proposition. Embed mockups, diagrams, or Figma links here for better context.
📐 Conceptual Model
Lay out the system’s key entities, how they relate, and how data flows between them. Includes a structured table of core concepts like accounts, logs, and events.
✅ Functional Requirements
A sortable database (table or kanban) for managing features by priority, type, and status. Helps break down scope into manageable pieces and track development.
📊 Success Metrics
Checklist for key performance indicators (KPIs) and metrics of success in the first year—like usage, retention, or revenue goals.
⏰ Timeline & Milestones
Timeline-based database to plan and track project phases and important dates such as MVP launch, testing rounds, or go-to-market milestones.
🗂️ Appendix
A flexible section for external resources, design files, API schemas, sample data, or any supplementary documentation.
✅ Who This Is For
Product managers creating specs for engineering teams

Startup founders outlining an MVP

Cross-functional teams aligning on scope and strategy

Agencies managing client software projects

templates.name: PRD – Opportunity Solution Tree
templates.description: Build your products with confidence and clarity, using this PRD template, based on the Opportunity Solution Tree framework by Teresa Torres.
templates.body_text:
Build products that people love with this PRD template, based on the "Opportunity Solution Tree" framework, created by Teresa Torres in her book "Continuous Discovery Habits".
This template streamlines product discovery for teams, by providing the Opportunity Solution Tree (OST) framework in a neat and organized document. Once you have your OST ready, you can jump in to each and every opportunity, and start using this template right away!
This template is divided into three sections:
Opportunity: where you can select an opportunity, assess its importance and impact.

Solutions: where you ideate on ways to address this opportunity, where you then can test them using the assumption test.

Conclusion: where you determine the solution that will be used, and how will you implement it to address your opportunity.

This template offers helpful tips to guide you along the way, as well as automating routine tasks like adding solutions and assumptions, to let you focus on what matters.

templates.name: [PRD101] PRD Toolkits & MVP Builder

templates.body_text:
The Problem for PMs:
Too often, great product ideas get stuck in the gray zone between “just a thought” and “ready to build.”
You might have:
A feature request… but no clear user flow.

A stakeholder’s vision… but no structured spec.

A dev team… asking for details you haven’t mapped out yet.

And when you try to write a PRD, it becomes:
Overwhelming to structure

Hard to align across roles (Sales, Eng, Legal, etc.)

Difficult to visualize — especially before Figma or Jira even enters the picture

✅ This Template Solves That.
This is a modular, prompt-powered Notion PRD framework built for PMs who want to:
Start from loose ideas, and shape them into structured PRDs
→ Use friendly prompts to map: scope, flows, users, permissions, edge cases.

Auto-generate UI-ready MVP specs
→ Convert your input into a Figma-style JSON — complete with flows, modules, access rules, and layout hints.

Visualize user journeys + guardrails with clarity
→ From onboarding to compliance gates to dashboards.

Collaborate cross-functionally with engineers, designers, and compliance/legal teams
→ Everyone knows their access, actions, and review steps.

🧠 What You’ll Get:
Structured template with AI-prompted for: Overview, User Roles, Flows, System Objectives, System Architecture, Modules, Modules & Feature Breakdown (spec‑level), Role Authority Table (by module)

Optional JSON export to plug into Figma / Lovable for auto-generated UI

Example cases for clarity

📦 Includes:
✅ Ready-to-use Notion PRD page

✅ Agent-friendly prompts for Notion AI

✅ Figma-compatible JSON format

✅ Case study examples for how to write good vs. bad inputs

✅ Checklist before handing off to design or dev

🔍 Example Case:
A PM defines a feature: “Individual Client Profiles”. Using this template, they structure:
Case output with figma: https://mclty.pse.is/87by2k
PRD Example:  https://mclty.pse.is/87bxpg
Scope & pain points (compliance risk, missing documents)

Users & permissions (Sales, Compliance, Admin)

Flow: from profile creation → document upload → compliance check

Module breakdown: Profile, Opportunity, Dashboard

Edge case: what if key docs are missing?

They input these sections → Notion AI Agent outputs JSON → which is then pasted into Figma (via plugin or Lovable) → to generate clickable mockups and flows.
🚀 In few hours, the team moves from “idea” → “ready to test UI”.
🧩 Best For:
Product Managers (especially solo PMs or startup teams)

Teams working with remote devs or async handoff

templates.name: Product Brief
templates.description: Use this Product Brief template to ensure alignment at the right moments and prevent any misaligned expectations around the problem, scope, and timeline. It also enables you to gather feedback, improve your thinking, and leverage any knowledge floating in the organization.
templates.body_text:
A product brief is a strategic document created in the discovery phase that captures the essence of a problem to be solved, helping teams align on objectives and gather stakeholder feedback before significant investment of resources.
What is a product brief?
A product brief is a concise, typically one-page document that defines a product opportunity in its early stages. It serves as a single source of truth that helps teams understand both the problem being addressed and why it matters. 
When creating a product brief, frame solutions and experiences as hypotheses since you likely haven't done enough research to be certain about them. This allows you to gather early feedback on whether your hypotheses sound directionally correct before investing more effort.
When to use a product brief?
A product brief should be created at specific points in the product development process:
At the beginning of a new initiative:
 before any external discovery work has been completed, when you're still forming your understanding of the problem space.

During the discovery phase:
 when your focus is on understanding the problem you aim to solve rather than defining detailed solutions.

When seeking early stakeholder input:
 to gather feedback from leadership and stakeholders that will help decide whether to pursue an opportunity further.

When alignment is needed:
 to ensure cross-functional teams share the same understanding of the opportunity, preventing misaligned expectations around scope and timeline.

Before investing significant resources:
 to validate the strategic value of an opportunity before committing extensive time and resources to it.

The product brief should reflect your best understanding of the problem you're tackling and what it might take to solve it. It should be created after reviewing existing content and context and speaking with internal parties who may have knowledge about the problem.

templates.body_text:
Most PRDs aren't useful because they are formed either too early or too late in the process. They often lack a sense of purpose which comes from situational context of the user and their needs. That's why I created a simple, user-centric blue print for a product requirements document that's to -the-point, easy to write and even easier to read.
Insight - an observed user need or pain

templates.name: Product Brief Library
templates.description: Use this Product Brief template to ensure alignment at the right moments and prevent any misaligned expectations around the problem, scope, and timeline. It also enables you to gather feedback, improve your thinking, and leverage any knowledge floating in the organization.
templates.body_text:
A product brief is a strategic document created in the discovery phase that captures the essence of a problem to be solved, helping teams align on objectives and gather stakeholder feedback before significant investment of resources.
What is a product brief?
A product brief is a concise, typically one-page document that defines a product opportunity in its early stages. It serves as a single source of truth that helps teams understand both the problem being addressed and why it matters. 
When creating a product brief, frame solutions and experiences as hypotheses since you likely haven't done enough research to be certain about them. This allows you to gather early feedback on whether your hypotheses sound directionally correct before investing more effort.
When to use a product brief?
A product brief should be created at specific points in the product development process:
At the beginning of a new initiative:
 before any external discovery work has been completed, when you're still forming your understanding of the problem space.

During the discovery phase:
 when your focus is on understanding the problem you aim to solve rather than defining detailed solutions.

When seeking early stakeholder input:
 to gather feedback from leadership and stakeholders that will help decide whether to pursue an opportunity further.

When alignment is needed:
 to ensure cross-functional teams share the same understanding of the opportunity, preventing misaligned expectations around scope and timeline.

Before investing significant resources:
 to validate the strategic value of an opportunity before committing extensive time and resources to it.

The product brief should reflect your best understanding of the problem you're tackling and what it might take to solve it. It should be created after reviewing existing content and context and speaking with internal parties who may have knowledge about the problem.
